text: The laparoscopic view shows the uterus with an anterior 3 cm type six myoma and a posterior 9 cm cyst. The surgeon opens the retroperitoneal space by retracting the sigmoid colon medially and cutting its peritoneal attachments. The retroperitoneal space is expanded, and connective tissues are removed to locate the uterine artery at its origin.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and removes surrounding fatty tissue to expose it. The umbilical artery is tracked, revealing tension in its upper position. The ureter is visualized medial to the umbilical artery, and its rotation is noted. The uterine artery is located between the ureter and the umbilical artery. The paravesical and pararectal fossae are visible, providing anatomical landmarks for the procedure.

The surgeon clips the uterine artery, a pulsatile vessel, within the lateral part of the paramedical fossa. The anatomy is reviewed, showing the uterus, umbilical artery, uterine artery, and uterine vein. The procedure is performed in the left retroperitoneal space, with careful dissection to avoid damaging veins, including a varicosity on the infundibulopelvic ligament. The surgeon uses forceps to tract veins and coagulates connective tissue, expanding the space around the external iliac vessel and posterior aspect of the round ligament.

The surgeon increases the space in the retroperitoneal area and identifies the umbilical artery, applying traction to visualize the vessel clearly. The ureter is located and its rotation is observed. The uterine artery is clipped at its origin. The procedure transitions to the laparoscopic myomectomy, focusing on a large posterior myoma. A longitudinal incision is made using bipolar devices, minimizing bleeding. The myoma is extracted from its bed using a 10mm laparoscopic forceps for strong traction, employing hydro-dissection to find the optimal cleavage plane and remove surrounding tissues.

The surgeon completes the extraction of a large myoma, placing it aside for morcellation. A large vein is visible in the surgical field. Using a 14mm needle, the surgeon performs an intracorporeal knotting technique, securing a hemostatic knot and embedding healthy myometrium into the myoma bed. The tissue is invaginated into the myoma bed, achieving effective hemostasis.

The surgeon invaginates the tissue, with the assistant temporarily locking the knot. The surgeon then ties a second and third knot to completely secure it. The first knot is a hemostatic knot, and additional knots are tied to ensure stability, placing healthy myometrium inside the myoma bed. The surgeon uses a double square knot technique, going three centimeters away from the scar, and employs a Vikryl one suture for strong traction. Visible ischemia near the edges of the myoma scar indicates well-tied stitches. Another stitch is placed near the punch of the blast, with the needle inserted from laterally to internally, ensuring optimal tissue invagination.

The surgeon ties the final knot on the myoma, ensuring the healthy tissue is invaginated into the myoma bed without deperitonization. The edges of the uterus show good ischemia. Fluid is removed from the pelvis to reduce the risk of postoperative adhesion. The anterior myoma is extracted using hydro-dissection and traction-countertraction techniques.

The surgeon places the second myoma into a glass container and closes the lid. Two stitches are applied using a double square knot technique, with the needle positioned far from the scar. A 14mm needle and Vikril one suture are used to ensure a strong knot.

The surgeon places the endoscopic bag in the pelvis for morcellation, ensuring the anterior and posterior scars show no bleeding and tight knots with visible ischemia. The small myomas are placed inside the bag, and the morcellator operates at 800 rounds per minute to fragment the tissue within the bag, reducing the risk of parasitic myomas. Small pieces of the myoma are visible inside the bag after morcellation.

The surgeon removes all liquid from the surgical field, confirming there are no remaining pieces of small myoma. The scar is checked for hemostasis, showing no bleeding. The uterine artery is visualized with a clip in place, indicating vascular control.
